text: One of the rich legacies that South Africa enjoys is that of powerful and prophetic preaching. This legacy includes notable church leaders such as the arch bishop Desmond Tutu, in their critique of the apartheid structures of inequality and injustice. Today many years later, South Africa continues to grapple with the inequality and the exclusion that characterises the huge and tragic poverty that afflicts over forty percent of our people. Based on insights from the social sciences and what we have learnt from the qualitative research we have done with poor people and communities, this article explores three conditions that need to be met in order to revive a truly prophetic kind of preaching and practice among the people of God, not only in South Africa but elsewhere in the world. These are firstly what we call ‘prophetic stance’ or ‘attitude’ toward the poor and to the challenge of poverty and exclusion that the poor represent. Secondly, there is what we call the homiletical ‘form’ of what constitutes a truly prophetic message and discourse which is quite different to ordinary ordinary preaching. Thirdly there is the need for a supportive and prophetic kind of community which will afford space for truly prophetic preaching and practice to flourish both within the Church and within the overall ecumenical body of Christ in the world. The church in our time lives in an era in which inequality, corruption and grandstanding and personal enrichment by the elite abound. If the church is to make sense in this time, she must find her voice to critique this inequality in the world and in our societal structures, and at the same time to speak a word of hope, promising a better life to our people.
